question: Explain the difference between low-fidelity and high-fidelity sketches. When would you use each?
expectedPoints: Low-fi: basic shapes, minimal lines, focuses on structure/layout
High-fi: includes colors, typography, refined details, resembles final product
Use low-fi in early ideation for rapid exploration
Use high-fi when design direction is established and needs visual refinement

question: Describe the 'Crazy 8s' sketching technique and its purpose in UI/UX design.
expectedPoints: Fold paper into 8 sections
Create one sketch per section in 8 minutes
Purpose: forces rapid ideation
Encourages creative problem-solving under time pressure
Generates multiple diverse ideas quickly

question: List and explain four benefits of sketching in the UI/UX design process.
expectedPoints: Rapid visualization of ideas
Low-cost and low-stakes exploration
Facilitates team communication
Enables quick iteration
Encourages creative risk-taking
Easy to discard and start over

question: What are annotation symbols in UI sketching? Give three examples and explain their usage.
expectedPoints: Annotation symbols are shorthand notations indicating interactions/functionality
Arrow: shows navigation or user flow direction
X or strikethrough: indicates deletion or removal
! or star: highlights important interactions or alerts
Circle/square on elements: indicates clickable/interactive items

question: Explain the relationship between sketching and the design thinking framework.
expectedPoints: Sketching supports the Ideation phase - rapid generation of ideas
Sketching helps in Prototyping - quick iteration of solutions
Enables iteration without time investment
Facilitates user-centered thinking through quick visualization
Bridges empathy/definition to concrete solutions

question: What is wireframing? How does it differ from detailed UI sketching?
expectedPoints: Wireframe: skeletal framework showing layout of key elements
Focuses on structure, hierarchy, and functionality
Does not include colors, detailed graphics, or final typography
Detailed sketching adds visual polish and refinement
Wireframing comes before detailed sketching in design process

question: Explain the '10-plus-10' sketching method and when it is most effective to use.
expectedPoints: First 10 minutes: sketch 10 variations of an idea
Select the best solution from the 10
Second 10 minutes: refine the chosen solution
Effective when a specific problem needs multiple solutions
Used after initial ideation to narrow down direction

question: You are a UI/UX designer working on a mobile app for a food delivery service. Describe how you would use sketching techniques throughout the design process. Include specific sketching methods, tools, and how you would incorporate feedback.
expectedPoints: Start with rapid sketching (Crazy 8s or 6-up) to explore multiple layouts
Create low-fi wireframes for key screens (home, menu, cart, checkout)
Add annotation symbols to indicate interactions and user flows
Conduct design critique sessions with stakeholders
Iterate based on feedback - sketch new versions quickly
Progress to high-fi sketches for visual refinement before digital design
Tools: sketchbook, fine-tip markers, stencils, square templates
Purpose: validate ideas early, reduce revision costs, align team understanding

question: Discuss why UI/UX designers should avoid jumping directly to digital tools. Explain how sketching serves as a thinking tool and facilitates collaboration.
expectedPoints: Digital tools require more time investment per iteration
Digital tools can limit creative exploration due to commitment
Sketching allows rapid visual thinking without technical constraints
Provides common visual language for diverse team members
Enables stakeholders without technical background to contribute feedback
Easy to modify, discard, and start fresh (low stakes)
Facilitates collaborative brainstorming sessions
Bridges communication gap between designers, developers, and clients
Reduces cost of changes early in design process

question: Compare and contrast sketching, wireframing, and prototyping. Include their purposes, level of detail, and when each is used in the design process.
expectedPoints: Sketching: quick, informal, exploration-focused, basic shapes, early stage
Wireframe: structured layout, shows hierarchy and placement, mid-stage between sketch and prototype
Prototype: interactive, simulates user experience, tests functionality, later stage
Detail level increases: sketch (minimal) → wireframe (structural) → prototype (functional)
Sketching: quantity and ideation
Wireframing: layout validation and structure
Prototyping: interaction testing and user feedback
All serve different purposes but are interconnected in design process

question: Design a sketch session plan for a team working on an e-commerce website checkout flow. Include the sketching techniques to be used, time allocation, and how feedback will be incorporated.
expectedPoints: Start with 5-minute warm-up thumbnail sketches
Use Crazy 8s or 10-plus-10 for checkout screen variations
Create 6-up exercise for different checkout layouts
Develop wireframes for selected concepts
Annotate sketches with user flow indicators
Conduct group critique session - vote on best approaches
Iterate sketches based on team feedback
Progress to high-fi sketches for final direction
Time estimate: 45-60 minutes for ideation, 30 minutes for critique
Document decisions on sketches for reference
